text: The surgeon carefully dissects and frees omental adhesions from the cecum to improve exposure. The omentum is visible over the liver and transverse colon. The surgeon then begins inferior mobilization, checking the ureter's proximity during this part of the dissection.
The surgeon performs an inferior lateral mobilization to improve visualization of the medial aspect of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and to clear medial lymph node tissue.

The surgeon performs inferior mobilization, checking the ureter and revealing the tattooed cancer in the mid-ascending colon. The dissection progresses along the root of the ileal mesentery towards the fourth portion of the duodenum and the ligament of Treitz. The duodenum is visualized as the cephalic extent of the dissection is reached, with adjustments in arm positioning to avoid contact with the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon continues dissection over the right kidney towards the inferior edge of the liver, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg with the right side elevated. Despite challenges in exposure due to patient positioning, the surgeon achieves adequate visualization to proceed with the mobilization.

The surgeon switches to a medial to lateral dissection, elevating the iliocolic pedicle and joining the medially and laterally dissected planes. The dissection proceeds down to the junction of the ileocolic artery and vein with the superior mesenteric artery and vein. The superior mesenteric vein is observed posterior and lateral to the superior mesenteric artery. The surgeon performs a CME dissection, identifying the ileocolic vein and working around the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon encounters bleeding after inadvertently injuring a branch of the loop of Henle. The ileocolic artery obstructs the view, but is repositioned to improve exposure. The surgeon lifts the tissue for better visualization and gains proximal and distal control around the injury site. Electrocautery is used on the head of the pancreas to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a clean sponge to manage the surgical field while continuing the dissection along the superior mesenteric artery, which is positioned anterior to the vein. The dissection progresses through the fatty tissue towards the right branch of the middle colic artery, with the superior mesenteric vein visible beneath the tissue.

The surgeon is taking the right branch of the middle colic artery, leaving some fatty tissue near the junction of the cecum and ascending colon tumor. The surface of the superior mesenteric artery is visible. The surgeon proceeds to take the transverse colon mesentery up to the mid-transverse colon, near the level of the false front ligament. The camera is positioned lower in the field, providing good exposure. A marginal branch is seen coming up to the colon wall.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the planned transection level of the colon, keeping it with the specimen. The camera is positioned in the left lower quadrant, providing a view of the hepatic flexure. The patient is repositioned using table motion to reduce reverse Trendelenburg, improving visibility. The surgeon proceeds to dissect through the gastrocolic and gastrohepatic omentum, carefully removing tissue from the stomach and pylorus while frequently checking proximity to these structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection over the hepatic flexure attachments of the colon, coming off the superior aspect of the right kidney. With further manipulation, the lateral mobilization is completed. The surgeon then brings in a stapler through the suprapubic port to staple the small bowel, preparing for specimen extraction through the same port.

The surgeon places the specimen above the liver and examines the dissection area, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and vein, with the vein appearing blue. The surgeon prepares for an isoparastaltic anastomosis, opting for a sewn side-to-side anastomosis of the right colon. A stay suture is placed at the far end to align the anastomosis and elevate the bowel for better exposure during suturing. The camera and working arms are optimally positioned, facilitating the procedure. The surgeon performs an outer back row of sutures using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, typical for extracorporeal or open surgery, and prepares to make an enterotomy.

The surgeon creates a colotomy and begins sewing the inner back row of the anastomosis, starting from the center and sewing away from themselves. The suture is transitioned to form the inner anterior row, inverting the mucosal edges. A second stitch is placed in the center, sewing towards the near side.

The surgeon is suturing the colotomy side, taking larger canal bites due to the thicker tissue compared to the enterotomy side. The sutures are brought towards the center and tied together, avoiding tying at the corners.

The surgeon completes a full circle two-layer anastomosis by sewing the original posterior outer suture over the top. The omentum is brought down to cover the mesenteric defects, and the specimen is extracted at the suprapubic port site.
